reports.title: Start an implementation loop manually on a spec
reports.summary: There is a real Rust start-loop pipeline in the repo: the CLI creates a dispatch through a shared app service, the orchestrator persists a pending dispatch plus an initial ready provision step, and the store writes the projection and lifecycle events atomically. But the declared API and TUI surfaces are still stubs, the MCP surface I found is not loop-start aware, and I found no B-0001 BDD proof, so this is not ready to count as fully implemented across its declared interfaces.

similar_behaviors.reason: Automatic start is the sibling path for beginning a loop on a spec.
Current loop state visibility is the companion read path for a started loop.
Dependency blocking is a prerequisite gate on the same start action.
A spec must be ready before a loop may start.
Canceling a loop shares the same loop lifecycle and status model.

implementation_evidence.summary: `dispatch create` builds a `CreateDispatchRequest` and sends it through the shared service, so the manual-start path is wired at the CLI surface.
The shared dispatch service converts the request into a domain command and calls `orchestrator.create_dispatch`.
`create_dispatch` mints a dispatch ID, checks policy, builds artifacts, persists them, and returns the created view.
The created loop includes a provision step at sequence 0 with `Ready` state and a pending dispatch view.
The store transaction inserts the dispatch projection, initial step, and both lifecycle events atomically under the replay guard.
Validation enforces a provision initial step, matching dispatch IDs, ready state, and valid replay-guard material.
The wire contract exposes dispatch status and step status/ready-state fields needed to show loop state to users.
The request converter validates and maps the manual-start payload into the domain `CreateDispatch` command.

documentation_evidence.summary: The behavior contract states that a solo-builder or team-builder can start an implementation loop on a selected spec.
Current repo-level verification tracking already classifies B-0001 as implemented, not yet asserted.
The system architecture requires CLI, API, MCP, TUI, and web surfaces to expose coherent state over the same event log and contracts.
The interfaces architecture says equivalent operations across CLI, API, TUI, and MCP must reach the same application service and event semantics.
Runtime is responsible for where assigned work runs and how isolated execution is prepared, but not for deciding the work itself.

architecture_evidence.summary: All public surfaces are supposed to share one contract model, which the current CLI-only wiring does not fully satisfy.
Public clients are expected to use the HTTP control plane consistently across CLI, API, TUI, and MCP.
CLI changes should go through typed API commands rather than local bypasses; the shared-service path is directionally correct.
The create path follows the control-plane pattern of policy check plus persisted events, which matches the accepted architecture for backend state changes.
Specs are expected to use Tanren-managed branches and isolated targets, which is consistent with the dispatch artifact model used here.

architecture_gaps.gap: `bin/tanren-api` and `bin/tanren-tui` are still stub binaries, so B-0001 is not available on the declared HTTP and terminal surfaces.
architecture_gaps.impact: The behavior does not satisfy the all-surface contract in the accepted interface architecture.

architecture_gaps.gap: The visible MCP tool surface in this checkout is methodology-oriented, so I do not see a loop-start tool exposed for agent clients.
architecture_gaps.impact: MCP does not currently meet the declared interface coverage for B-0001.

architecture_gaps.gap: The current start pipeline is dispatch-centric and does not yet show spec-ready or dependency gates in the code I found.
architecture_gaps.impact: B-0017 and B-0019 are not visibly enforced by the manual-start path yet.

gaps.gap: No B-0001 BDD feature exists in `tests/bdd`, so there is no positive or falsification witness yet.
gaps.impact: The behavior is not asserted and remains vulnerable to regressions.

gaps.gap: The declared API, TUI, and MCP surfaces are not wired for the manual-start action in the checkout I inspected.
gaps.impact: Users and agents cannot reach the behavior through every supported interface yet.

gaps.gap: The current start path does not visibly enforce spec readiness or dependency usability before creating the loop.
gaps.impact: The behavior still depends on adjacent readiness/dependency work before it can be considered complete.

gaps.gap: The code I found returns dispatch state, but I did not find a dedicated spec-lifecycle mutation that proves the spec itself is visibly running immediately after start.
gaps.impact: The user-facing outcome described in the behavior contract is not fully demonstrated from the available code alone.

reports.title: Automatically start eligible ready work when serial execution is configured
reports.summary: Tanren has the adjacent primitives for manual loop start, ready-spec eligibility, dependency blocking, and serial execution, but I found no end-to-end path that automatically starts the next eligible ready spec when a loop finishes. The autostart trigger, project-level toggle, and visible trigger/eligibility provenance are still missing from the current contract and service surfaces.

similar_behaviors.reason: Manual start is the same lifecycle entry point autostart would reuse.
Autostart must not create a second concurrent loop on the same spec.
Dependency blocking applies to autostart as well as manual starts.
Only ready specs can be auto-started.
Backlog specs are excluded from autostart eligibility.
Users need an explanation for why the next work item was chosen.
Autostart is constrained by autonomy policy.
This is the adjacent continue-until-blocker automation boundary.

implementation_evidence.summary: `start_task` is a manual mutation path that emits `TaskStarted` only when an explicit start command is issued.
`complete_task` advances the current task to implemented/completed but does not hand off to a subsequent ready spec.
`plan_transition` only computes the next transition inside the current spec's task/check/walk flow.

documentation_evidence.summary: The accepted behavior explicitly requires completion-triggered automatic starts, visible trigger provenance, and a disable toggle.
The current verification classification puts every accepted behavior except B-0001, B-0003, B-0021, and B-0058 in the unimplemented bucket, which includes B-0002.
Only Ready specs may have a loop started on them; Blocked specs cannot start.

architecture_evidence.summary: All public clients share one contract model, so any autostart trigger must be observable across CLI, API, MCP, TUI, and web.
Orchestration owns spec lifecycle and active-spec routing, which is where a next-spec autostart decision would belong.
Runtime already treats `shared_serial` as the sequential execution baseline, so the execution substrate exists for serial work.

architecture_gaps.gap: The current contract shapes do not carry an autostart trigger or eligibility rationale.
architecture_gaps.impact: B-0002's visible-start requirement cannot be represented cleanly in the current event or response model.

architecture_gaps.gap: There is no project/scope configuration field that enables or disables completion-based autostart.
architecture_gaps.impact: The behavior cannot be controlled as a first-class policy choice for a project.

gaps.gap: No scheduler/orchestration path starts the next eligible ready spec when another loop finishes.
gaps.impact: Manual loop start exists, but the end-to-end product behavior does not.

gaps.gap: No B-0002-specific BDD feature or scenario was found under `tests/bdd/features`.
gaps.impact: The behavior cannot yet be asserted or regression-checked.

suggested_next_items.title: Add completion-triggered autostart plumbing for ready specs
suggested_next_items.rationale: Wire a daemon/orchestration path that starts the next eligible ready spec when a loop finishes and serial execution/autonomy policy allows it.

suggested_next_items.rationale: Prove that the next eligible ready spec starts automatically after a preceding loop completes.

suggested_next_items.rationale: Prove autostart does not fire for backlog, blocked, or already-running specs.

notes: No B-0002 feature file or scenario was found under `tests/bdd/features`.
The codebase already models serial execution as a runtime baseline, but not as a project-level autostart policy.

The repo is still short of the accepted interface architecture because API/TUI entrypoints are stubs and MCP is still stdio/sqlite-shaped rather than the HTTP-based control-plane contract, so this is close but not yet implementation-complete for the behavior as written.

reports.title: Cancel a loop
reports.summary: The core cancel path is present and follows the accepted Tanren layering: transport contracts flow into app services, then orchestration, then the state store/event log, with authorization, transactional cancellation of pending steps, and cancellation attribution recorded in events. The remaining gaps are interface coverage and executable proof: there is no BDD feature for B-0058, the API entrypoint is still a stub in this checkout, and the MCP surface shown here does not expose a cancel-loop tool.

similar_behaviors.reason: Shares the same implementation-loop lifecycle and dispatch-based surface.
Cancel finality depends on the same loop state model that exposes running/paused/cancelled state.
Pause/resume is the closest lifecycle sibling and clarifies cancellation as the terminal alternative.
Cancellation must be attributed and visible in action history.
Runtime interruption handling overlaps with cancellation and recovery semantics.

implementation_evidence.summary: DispatchService exposes a cancel entrypoint that converts the contract request and forwards it to orchestration for all transport clients.
Orchestrator::cancel_dispatch enforces cancel policy, hides unauthorized cancels as not-found, emits DispatchCancelled, and delegates atomic cancellation to the store.
StateStore::cancel_dispatch validates and runs the cancel transaction in one database transaction, then normalizes contention errors.
The cancel transaction consumes replay state, checks current status, cancels pending steps in batches, updates dispatch status to cancelled, and appends the lifecycle event.
PolicyEngine has an explicit cancel-authorization path that scopes the actor against the dispatch's ownership context.
DispatchCancelled and StepCancelled carry actor and reason data, which preserves cancellation attribution in the canonical event stream.
The CLI exposes dispatch cancel as a first-class mutation and prints a cancelled result after the service call succeeds.
The public contract defines a typed CancelDispatchRequest for transport surfaces.

documentation_evidence.summary: The behavior contract states that a running or paused implementation loop can be cancelled, cancellation is final, and the loop becomes available for a fresh start.
The current verification projection classifies B-0058 as implemented but not yet asserted.
Behavior is the unit of product meaning, and all durable state transitions flow through the event log and shared surface contracts.
All public clients must share one contract model, and equivalent operations across CLI, API, MCP, TUI, and web must reach the same semantics.
Spec lifecycle includes a cancelled terminal state for work that stops before completion.
State architecture requires canonical append-only events and explicit idempotency, which matches the cancellation implementation path.
Runtime owns cancellation, timeout, retry, and recovery behavior for execution assignments.
Operations treats cancellation as an explicit selectable effect rather than the default consequence of pause or drain.

architecture_evidence.summary: Equivalent operations across public surfaces must reach the same application service and produce the same event semantics.
The implementation uses typed events, transactional append, and replay-safe projection updates consistent with the state architecture.
Cancel flow stays in orchestrator logic rather than bypassing policy or store layers.
The state store keeps cancellation inside a typed transactional store boundary instead of direct projection mutation.

architecture_gaps.gap: The HTTP API entrypoint is still a stub in this checkout, so the documented API surface does not yet demonstrate cancel-loop wiring end to end.
architecture_gaps.impact: The behavior cannot be counted as fully surfaced across all declared interfaces.

architecture_gaps.gap: The MCP handler/tool registry shown here is methodology-only and does not expose a cancel-loop tool.
architecture_gaps.impact: Agent-facing cancellation is not shown through the declared MCP surface.

gaps.impact: The repo has implementation evidence but lacks positive and falsification witnesses for product-level proof.

gaps.gap: The cancellation-attribution requirement is recorded in events, but there is no dedicated loop-history read path for B-0014 visible in the implementation evidence.
gaps.impact: The action-history clause in B-0058 remains only partially evidenced.

suggested_next_items.rationale: Prove the happy path that a running loop becomes cancelled and unavailable for resume.

reports.title: Disconnect or replace an external tracker

B-0091 therefore looks like partial foundation, not implemented behavior.

The methodology service only carries `issue_provider` as a runtime config string defaulted to GitHub; that is not a persisted external-tracker connection record.
The public methodology tool surface includes `CreateIssue` but no tracker connect/disconnect/replace tool.
Installer logic only derives issue-provider noun phrases from configuration; it does not manage tracker connection state.

reports.title: Stop or recover interrupted execution
reports.summary: The repository has strong runtime primitives for cancellation, stale-step recovery, session-resume capability, and dispatch-level cancel flows, but not an end-to-end user-facing interrupted-execution recovery path. This is partial foundation rather than implemented.

similar_behaviors.reason: Directly covers interrupted worker-session resume/reconciliation and the original-assignment linkage this behavior depends on.
Covers honoring cancellation and access revocation, including recording whether affected work needs recovery.
Shares the runtime-failure recovery surface and duplicate-visible-work constraint for retry/recovery paths.
Provides the stop/cancel half of the user-facing interruption story, though for loops rather than runtime substrate.
Operational health visibility is the adjacent surface for seeing stuck workers, queues, and execution targets.

implementation_evidence.summary: Harness requirements include a first-class `session_resume` dimension, so resumable execution can be required by policy.
Harness capabilities advertise `session_resume` and preflight denies adapters that cannot satisfy the requested resume level.
Execution results persist a `session_resumed` flag, which gives the runtime a way to record when a session continued after interruption.
The job queue supports heartbeat-based liveness and `recover_stale_steps`, which reclaims stale running work after worker loss.
Cancel dispatch is handled transactionally and emits cancellation events while updating dispatch and step state atomically.
The orchestrator exposes `cancel_dispatch`, enforces policy, and records a `DispatchCancelled` event before store mutation.
The CLI has a public `dispatch cancel` command that reaches the shared dispatch service.

documentation_evidence.summary: The behavior intent requires understandable state after cancellation, crash, or lease loss, plus no duplicate visible work on recovery.
B-0105 is cataloged in the accepted runtime-substrate area alongside the adjacent runtime-failure and worker-access behaviors.
All accepted behaviors not listed as implemented/asserted remain verification_status `unimplemented`; B-0105 is not in the implemented set.
Runtime owns cancellation, retry, recovery, reconciliation, and worker-scoped temporary access at the system level.
Recovery is explicitly reconciliation-based, including interrupted sessions and expired leases.
Runtime handles infrastructure retry and execution recovery, and interrupted assignments reconcile into retry, restart, reassignment, or escalation.
The TUI is expected to surface recovery and incident workflows through the shared API contract model.
State recovery is replay-oriented and includes reconciling workers, queues, and leases from durable state.
Observation models runtime cancellation and recovery state as first-class signals.

architecture_evidence.summary: The accepted system architecture assigns cancellation, retry, recovery, and reconciliation to Runtime.
Runtime recovery is designed around reconciliation of interrupted sessions and expired leases.
Interrupted assignments are expected to reconcile into retry, restart, reassignment, or escalation, matching the behavior's recovery framing.
All public surfaces share commands, queries, subscriptions, exports, capability metadata, and diagnostics, so recovery must be surfaced through the shared contract model.
Operational recovery is replay-oriented, with workers, queues, and leases reconciled from durable state.
Provider integrations explicitly own authorization-failure recovery and reconciliation of drifted external targets, which is consistent with runtime recovery paths.

architecture_gaps.gap: No dedicated public recover-interrupted-execution command or tool surface is visible; the repo currently exposes cancellation and internal stale-step recovery, but not a coherent user-directed recovery workflow.
architecture_gaps.impact: B-0105 cannot yet be exercised end to end from CLI/API/MCP/TUI as a distinct stop-or-recover action.

architecture_gaps.gap: Interrupted-execution outcomes are represented by generic dispatch/step cancellation, failure, and stale-step reclamation primitives rather than a dedicated visible recovery model.

gaps.gap: Recovery is mostly implicit today: cancellation exists, stale workers can be reclaimed, and session-resume metadata is tracked, but the repo lacks a single user-facing interrupted-execution recovery flow.

reports.title: Explain which standards influenced a decision
reports.summary: Tanren has standards loading, relevance selection, and adherence plumbing, but no decision-level contract or read path that explains which standards influenced a specific planning, shaping, execution, or review decision.

implementation_evidence.summary: Filtered standards listing returns each included standard with an explicit inclusion reason, but only for relevance selection, not a specific decision.
Relevance reasons are computed from touched files, languages, domains, or universal applicability, providing the main explanation primitive currently available.

Adherence findings resolve a concrete standard from the runtime registry and use its importance to enforce severity rules.

The MCP tool registry exposes read-only relevant-standards access for agents and tool clients.

test_evidence.summary: B-0071 proves runtime standards load and fail explicitly; this is adjacent substrate coverage, not decision influence behavior.
B-0068 proves standards are preserved and restored on reinstall; useful substrate evidence, but not B-0151 coverage.
B-0069 proves missing standards are detected as drift, which supports standards provenance but not decision explanations.

documentation_evidence.summary: The behavior explicitly requires user-visible explanation of which standards influenced a planning or execution decision.
Related behavior establishes the existing pattern for explainable decision rationale at the work-selection level.
Related behavior defines conflict detection before work starts, which is adjacent to influence explanation.
Architecture says adherence findings cite the violated standard and only evaluate relevant standards.
Observation is defined as an explainable, provenance-aware read-side layer, which is the right surface for decision explanations.

architecture_evidence.summary: The accepted architecture assigns `standards-evolution` to Planning, so this behavior belongs in the planning/standards method family.

Effective configuration must explain source scope, inheritance, override, and constraints, including standards-profile adoption.

Standards relevance is a first-class quality-control concern, but the architecture stops at relevance rather than decision-level provenance.

The list_relevant_standards response carries inclusion_reason, which explains why a standard was selected, but no fields for conflict, blocking, or override routing.

test_evidence.summary: Search found no B-0152 feature or step coverage in the BDD tree.

reports.title: Avoid duplicate visible work on retry
reports.summary: The repository has strong replay-safety primitives for retries: generic idempotent mutation replay, step/job-queue retry counters, and visible retry metadata in responses. What is still missing is an end-to-end runtime worker-assignment retry contract that reuses the original assignment and exposes attempt-by-attempt provenance for B-0250.

similar_behaviors.reason: The broader runtime retry behavior covers the same no-duplicate-visible-work requirement and worker-level retry visibility.
Interrupted-session reconciliation is the closest assignment-linked retry/recovery behavior and explicitly keeps retries tied to the original assignment.
Client request replay uses idempotency and original identity context to avoid duplicate visible work after failure.
Cancellation and recovery share the same original-work linkage problem space and influence whether retry creates new visible work.

implementation_evidence.summary: Generic idempotent mutation replay is implemented with request hashing, reservation leases, and stored outcomes, so repeated attempts can return the original result instead of creating a second effect.
Step failure with retry resets the same row back to pending and increments retry_count instead of creating a new visible work row.
StepResponse exposes retry_count to public clients, which makes repeated attempts visible in the contract rather than hidden in the store.
Phase outcomes are recorded with spec, task, and agent-session identity, giving retries a durable attempt trail even when the outcome is replayed idempotently.
Investigate-driven remediation resets guards on the same task under idempotency and records a human-readable reason, which reuses the original work item instead of cloning it.

documentation_evidence.summary: The behavior intent explicitly requires retrying assigned work without creating duplicate visible work, and the observable outcomes call for reuse of the original assignment, work item, and idempotency context.
